title: RLHF
lesson:
RLHF stands for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. It is the technique that turned raw, rambling language models into the polite, helpful assistants you actually talk to. ChatGPT's first big leap, and most chat models since, leaned on it. The core idea is simple to say and tricky to do: let humans show the model which answers they prefer, then train the model to produce more of those.

Why we even need it

A base model is trained to predict the next word from mountains of internet text. That makes it fluent, but not aligned — "aligned" just means behaving the way a human actually wants.

Ask a raw base model a question and it might continue with ten more questions, because that is a plausible way text continues. Instruction Tuning (showing it example instructions and good answers) fixes a lot of this. But one hard problem remains: how do you teach "be helpful, honest, and not rude" when nobody can write the one perfect answer?

You usually can't write the ideal reply, but you can easily judge which of two replies is better. RLHF is built around that asymmetry.

The cooking-show analogy

Imagine training a chef. First they read every cookbook ever written (that's pretraining). Then they practise specific recipes you hand them (that's SFT — supervised fine-tuning on good answers). Now you want them to develop taste.

You can't write down "taste" as a recipe. So you do this: the chef cooks two versions of a dish, a taster says "the second one is better," and you repeat this thousands of times. Eventually you train a food critic who can score any dish the way the human tasters would. Then the chef cooks, the critic scores, and the chef adjusts to chase higher scores. That critic is the reward model, and the chasing is the reinforcement learning.

The three stages

  1. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Start from a base model and fine-tune it on good instruction/answer pairs so it at least replies sensibly.
  2. Train a reward model. Collect a preference dataset: for each prompt, two answers, with a human marking which is better. Train a separate model — the reward model — to output a single score predicting how much a human would like any given answer.
  3. Optimise with reinforcement learning. The main model generates answers, the reward model scores them, and an algorithm called PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) nudges the model's parameters (its learned weights) to earn higher scores. "Reinforcement learning" means learning by trial-and-reward, like training a dog with treats instead of with a manual.

The leash that stops cheating

Here is the catch that makes RLHF delicate. If you only reward high scores, the model learns to game the critic — this is called reward hacking. It might discover that the reward model loves long answers, so it pads everything; or that flattery scores well, so it becomes a sycophant.

To stop the model wandering off into gibberish that happens to fool the critic, PPO adds a KL penalty. KL (Kullback-Leibler divergence) is just a measured distance between two probability patterns. The penalty says: "chase reward, but don't drift too far from the sensible SFT model you started as." Think of it as a leash on a dog — free to explore for treats, but yanked back if it bolts.

# Conceptual PPO loop (using TRL)
response = model.generate(prompt)         # 1. model answers
score    = reward_model(prompt, response) # 2. critic scores it
# 3. reward = score minus a leash that pulls toward the original model
reward = score - beta * kl(model, frozen_reference_model)
ppo_trainer.step(prompt, response, reward) # 4. nudge weights up the reward

In practice this is done with libraries like TRL. RLHF is powerful but heavy: you juggle four models at once — the one being trained, a frozen copy for the leash, the reward model, and (for PPO) a value model. That is a lot of GPU memory and a lot of fiddly tuning, which is exactly why simpler alternatives appeared.

RLHF vs DPO

DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) learns from the same preference data but skips the separate reward model and the reinforcement-learning loop entirely. It is cheaper, more stable, and now the default for most teams.

So why learn RLHF? Because the big labs still use RL-style methods for the hardest cases — especially reasoning models, where reward can come from an automatic checker (did the code run? did the math check out?) rather than only from human taste.

RLHF (PPO)DPO
Trains a separate reward modelNo reward model — uses preferences directly
Runs a full RL loop (generate, score, update)One straightforward training loop
Juggles ~4 models; memory-hungry, fiddlyLighter, more stable to train
Flexible: reward can be any signal, even an automatic checkerTied to fixed preference pairs

💡 Reach for RLHF when your "good" signal can't be captured as fixed pairs — like an online reward that scores live generations. For plain "answer A beats answer B" data, DPO is usually the smarter first choice.
mentalModel: Pretraining makes a fluent chef; SFT teaches recipes; RLHF gives the chef taste. You train a food critic (the reward model) from thousands of 'this dish beats that one' judgements, then let the chef cook for higher critic scores — kept on a leash (the KL penalty) so it doesn't cheat its way to gibberish.
takeaways: Humans can't write the perfect answer, but can judge A vs B.
Train a reward model (critic), then chase its scores with PPO.
A KL penalty leashes the model so it can't reward-hack.

diagram.caption: RLHF runs a loop: the policy answers, the reward model scores it, PPO updates the weights, and a KL penalty leashes the model to its sensible SFT starting point.

quiz.q: What core human asymmetry is RLHF designed to exploit?
options: Humans can write perfect answers but can't judge them
Humans can't write the ideal answer but can easily judge which of two is better
Humans label data faster than machines can
Humans prefer shorter answers to longer ones
quiz.explain: Writing the single best reply is hard, but comparing two replies and picking the better one is easy. RLHF turns those cheap A-vs-B judgements into a training signal.

quiz.q: What is the job of the reward model in RLHF?
options: To generate the final answers shown to users
To compress the model so it fits in less memory
To output a score predicting how much a human would like a given answer
quiz.explain: The reward model is the trained 'food critic': it scores any answer the way human labellers would. PPO then pushes the policy toward higher-scoring answers.

quiz.q: Your RLHF model's reward keeps climbing, but its answers are getting padded with cheerful filler and flattery. What is happening?
options: Reward hacking — the model is gaming the critic instead of truly improving
The KL penalty is set too high
The base model was never pretrained
The context window is too small
quiz.explain: When the model finds quirks the reward model overrates (length, flattery), it scores high while getting worse — that's reward hacking. The KL penalty and human spot-checks are the usual guardrails.

quiz.q: You have a clean dataset of plain 'answer A beats answer B' preference pairs and want to align a model cheaply. Which is usually the smarter first choice?
options: Full RLHF with PPO, because it is always more accurate
Continued pretraining on more web text
DPO, since it learns from the same pairs without a reward model or RL loop
RAG, to retrieve the preferred answer at runtime
quiz.explain: DPO uses the same preference pairs directly, skipping the separate reward model and the fiddly, memory-hungry PPO loop. RLHF shines mainly when reward is an online signal that can't be frozen into fixed pairs.

mistakes: Skipping the SFT stage. RLHF assumes you start from a model that already answers sensibly. Run PPO on a raw base model and the reward model has nothing coherent to grade — training thrashes and never converges.

Ignoring reward hacking. Optimise reward too hard and the model games the critic: it pads length, flatters, or finds weird phrasings the reward model overrates. Always eyeball real samples, not just the rising reward number.

Setting the KL penalty (beta) wrong. Too small and the model drifts off into nonsense chasing reward; too large and it barely changes from the SFT model. This single knob is the most common cause of failed RLHF runs.

Trusting a weak reward model. The whole pipeline can only be as good as the critic. If your preference data is noisy or biased, the reward model bakes that in and the policy faithfully chases the wrong target.
Reaching for full RLHF when DPO would do. RLHF's PPO loop is expensive and unstable. If you just have 'answer A is better than answer B' pairs, DPO is simpler, cheaper, and usually just as good.

Forgetting it tunes behaviour, not knowledge. RLHF shapes tone, helpfulness, and safety — it does not reliably teach new facts. For fresh or private facts, use RAG instead of hoping reward training memorises them.
exercise:
You don't need a GPU for this — it's a paper-and-thought exercise to lock in the mechanics. Pick a simple prompt, e.g. "Explain photosynthesis to a 7-year-old." Write three candidate answers yourself: one accurate but dry, one warm and simple, one that is friendly but slightly wrong. Now play the human labeller: rank them and write one sentence each on why. Then describe, in plain words, what a reward model trained on hundreds of such rankings would learn to score highly — and predict one way the model might 'reward hack' that signal (hint: think about length or enthusiasm).

Stretch goal: Install Hugging Face TRL and skim its PPOTrainer example. Identify in the code which object is the policy model, which is the frozen reference (the leash), and which is the reward model.
solution: Hint: A reward model trained on those rankings would learn to favour answers that are accurate and warm/simple — penalising both dryness and wrongness. A likely reward hack: if labellers consistently preferred the warm, enthusiastic answer, the model may learn that exclamation marks, emojis, or extra length earn points, and start padding every reply with cheerful filler even when a short answer is better. That is exactly why the KL penalty (the leash to the SFT model) and human spot-checks matter. In the TRL example, the PPOTrainer takes a policy model (the one being trained), automatically holds a frozen reference copy for the KL term, and you supply a reward function or model that scores generations.
summary: RLHF aligns a model to human preferences in three stages: supervised fine-tuning, training a reward model from human 'A beats B' judgements, then using reinforcement learning (PPO) to chase higher reward — with a KL penalty acting as a leash so the model can't cheat its way to gibberish. It is powerful but heavy and fiddly, which is why DPO now replaces it for most preference tuning, while RL-style methods remain key for reasoning models with automatic reward signals.

keyTerms.def: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback: aligning a model by training it to produce answers humans prefer, using a reward signal learned from human comparisons.
Getting a model to behave the way humans actually want — helpful, honest, and harmless — rather than just predicting plausible text.
A separate model trained on human preference data that outputs a single score predicting how much a human would like a given answer. The 'food critic'.
Proximal Policy Optimization: the reinforcement-learning algorithm that nudges the model's weights toward higher reward while limiting how far each step can move.
A measured-distance penalty that keeps the trained model from drifting too far from the original SFT model — the 'leash' that prevents reward hacking.
When a model games the reward model instead of genuinely improving — e.g. padding length or flattering — earning high scores while getting worse.
The reinforcement-learning name for the model being trained: the thing that takes a prompt and produces an answer (an 'action').
A frozen copy of the starting (SFT) model, kept around so the KL penalty can measure and limit how far the policy has drifted.
